only about a half dozen comic books.
As already noted, Barks's work was published primarily under the Dell and Gold Key labels. Dell — an independent company, and not a subsidiary of Western — published most of the comic books that Western produced until 1962, when Western began publishing all of its comic books itself, under the Gold Key label. I have noted the labels under which a comic book was published, and also when the change in labels took place. When publication of a particular title was stopped by Dell and resumed by Gold Key, starting over again with No. 1, the listings are separate, rather than continuous.
**FOR EACH COMIC** book in which Barks's work appeared, I have listed the issue number, the date, and the total number of pages, including covers. The first two items require some explanation.
Issue numbers — Much of Barks's work appeared in Dell comic books that were originally called "Four Color Comics," and were numbered in that series. The Four Color designation was dropped after Four Color Comic No. 101; thereafter, these irregularly published comic books could be identified by the initials "O.S." — for "one-shot" — preceding the issue number at the bottom of the first inside page. For example, this is what appears at the bottom of the first page of a 1949 issue of *Donald Duck* — "W\.D.O.S.#223-494." Translated, that means "Walt Disney one-shot No. 223, April 1949." In the bibliography, I have listed all of these one-shots as "Four Colors," even when they do not bear that designation on their covers, in order to clearly distinguish them from issues in regular monthly, bimonthly, or quarterly series.
Dates — Most of the aforementioned Four Color comic books were not dated, except for the copyright date and the code date at the bottom of the first page; it is this code date that I have listed, in parentheses, since it tells more precisely when a comic book was published
than the copyright date alone does. For example, *Donald Duck* Four Color No. 223 was published when Dell comic books dated April 1949 were being published; this means that it appeared late in February or early in March 1949, since Dell comics typically appeared on newsstands three to six weeks before the cover dates.
Some comics in the Four Color series did have dates on their covers or in their indicia (a term to be defined later) in addition to their code dates. *Donald Duck* was published on a bi-monthly schedule within the Four Color series in 1951 and 1952. Some later Four Color comics had cover dates even though they were not published regularly; for example, *Grandma Duck's Farm Friends* Four Color No. 1161 is dated Feb.-Apr. 1961 even though that title was never published quarterly. To complicate matters further, those cover dates were sometimes at odds with the code dates, so that the code date for Four Color No. 1161 is (January) 1961. (The cover dates apparently were advanced in this fashion in an effort to keep the comic books on sale longer.) In such cases, I have listed the code date as well as the cover date — again, with the idea of giving the reader a better idea of when a comic book actually went on sale.
Gold Key's code dates are on the covers of each issue under the Gold Key seal, so that "302," for example, stands for either February 1963 or February 1973. A Gold Key one-shot or quarterly typically was put on sale three months before its cover code date, a bi-monthly two months ahead, a monthly one or two months ahead.
(To muddy the waters still further, Gold Key dropped all dates except code dates from all of its comic books for a few issues in early 1973, even while continuing to publish them on regular schedules. This was evidently tied to a campaign to sell more comics in batches, in plastic bags. Topical material — like Christmas stories — was rooted out of the comics at the same time. In such cases, in the
